alfabetizzazione-delle-reti-come-compren.english: Alphabetization of networks: How to understand, design and read visual relational models. Models help us understand complexity by offering simplified structures that make otherwise invisible social dynamics readable. In this context, networks are distinguished by their ability to represent relational information: nodes and connections reduce society to actors and connections, bringing out patterns that often remain hidden in our eyes. From the 18th century the networks have passed from being curious mathematical objects to essential tools in many disciplines. The first network visualizations revealed patterns of friendship in class highlighting social ties, while today computational studies map a great diversity of data, from cooking recipes to scientific collaborations. With their visual grammar, the networks today invite us to compare, classification and interpretation in a multitude of disciplines. However, their diffusion also brings risks: the networks can be exchanged for objective spaces, the central nodes for the most important, and the density admired for the aesthetics. To face these visual challenges we need a new form of literacy. Literacy of networks can be defined as the ability of literacy, and the ability of literacy of the networks can be defined by defining them. The article explores three dimensions of the networks: the design choices that shape the meaning, the spatial thought that guides the interpretation and the experimental projects that transform visualization into practical performance. By placing networks at the intersection between the design of information, critical research and cultural practice, the article argues that spreading the literacy of networks is essential to confront the design of knowledge in today's social fabric. The notion of literacy of networks arises from the recognition of networks as one of the most widespread ways to shape and visualize the relational complexity in today's society (Lima 2011). Speaking of literacy means considering networks not only as analytical tools but also as cultural artifacts, i.e. forms of writing that require technical and interpretative skills. Halfway between visual literacy that prepares for deciphering images and literacy of data that provides the ability to analyze the statistics, the literacy of networks can be defined as the ability to understand, to design and to read these networks, this can be defined as the ability to be constructed networks. It involves the identification of images and the literacy of data that provides the ability to analyze the networks can be defined as the ability to understand, to be understood, to be understood and to be able to be able to be able to be able to develop and able to be able to be able to develop and able to be able to be able to develop this role. It is practical, because it requires mastery of the techniques and tools that translate social dimensions into nodes and connections, but it is also cultural, because it recognizes that the networks are not neutral mirrors of reality. The visualizations of networks are to all effects constructed artifacts, which enclose decisions, priorities and assumptions. A network can represent friendships in a school, quotations in a field of research or images in a museum collection, but in any case expresses a specific vision of the world: what is considered a node, what counts as a link, which properties are highlighted. Being alphabetized to the networks means knowing how to decode these choices, wondering how the model has been constructed and interpreting the visual conventions with critical eye. The notion of network literacy can be better understood if we place it between literacy to data and visual literacy. Catherine D'Ignatius has supported the idea of a Dondis has defined visual literacy as the universal ability to compose, interpret and share meanings through visual forms, a fundamental competence as to verbal literacy (Dondis [1973] 1975). Given these premises, the literacy of networks is placed at the crossroads between these two traditions. As literacy to data, it requires technical and critical awareness on how information is structured; as visual literacy, it puts at the centre the interpretation, communication and social power of images. But it is distinguished by the specific attention to the relational model: the way in which nodes and links codify meanings, how design decisions shape interpretation and how the public sees networks as cultural artifacts. In this sense, the literacy of networks extends both traditions, proposing that, in an era in which knowledge is increasingly organized in relational way, they serve skills not only to read data and images, but also to compare with the visual grammars for this type of literacy, suggesting that, in an era in which knowledge is increasingly organized in relational way, they serve competences not only to read data and images, but also to compare themselves with the visual grammars for this type of literacy emerges when we observe the networks in the life of all the calendars of the days. The scientific research also relies on the networks: maps of quotes and graphs of co-authorship visualize the relationships between authors and fields of study, allowing us to trace the constructivist evolution of knowledge (Noichl 2019). These diversified uses show how the networks have passed from specialized tools to daily cultural forms. But their omnipresence also implies risks. Many readers interpret the networks as infallible representations, exchanging distance for intrinsic importance or assuming that the central nodes are objectively more influential. Without a literacy that unites a minimum of technical mastery and critical interpretation, the public risks being deceived precisely by the tools designed to understand the complexity. In this sense, the literacy of the networks is an essential civic competence, which allows designers and citizens to orient themselves in the increasingly relational fabric of information (Eco 2009) the theme of this issue of the magazine reinterviews us to rethink the form of the code. Traditionally, the code organizes the knowledge in sequence, page after page, producing an orderly and linear narration (Echo 2009). In this sense, the networks do not abolish the code, but extend it in a form capable of welcoming complexity, multiplicity and non-linearity. An emblematic example is the AIME project by Bruno Latour (2013), where paragraphs, references and keywords are interwoven into a digital interface that transforms the book into a relational environment, putting the connections in the text in the foreground (Ricci et al. 2017). Cultivating the literacy of the networks therefore means learning to read this new code: treating nodes and bonds as pages and paragraphs, interpreting clusters as chapters, and understanding that the act of reading is an act of navigation. Design plays a central role in this new critical literacy. Algorithms of layouts, palettes of color, interactive functions all these elements guide the way in which the networks are perceived and interpreted. A layout force-dirrected activity can group similar nodes in this critically enfacial literacy. Those who observe must learn to wonder what assumptions support visualization: because some data are included or others excluded, because centrality can have a relative importance, or because a cluster seems more coherent than it really is. An alphabetized approach recognizes that networks can seduce with aesthetics, deceive through algorithmic bias or simplify excessively complex realities. By joining design and critical interpretation, the literacy of networks cultivates a double vision: on the one hand the ability to build networks in a responsible way and on the other the ability to read them carefully. It teaches us to see the networks not only as technical diagrams, but as rhetorical devices, persuasive in form as well as in content. To follow, the section History of the networks outlines the historical picture of the networks. Design of networks analyzes how the choices in the preparation of data, layouts and instruments mute what the networks communicate, revealing how the meaning is encoded through design. The spatial thinking focuses on interpretation, focusing on how the networks are read in the bidimensional space, how to be oriented, how to the centrality, how the centrality, how they can also deconstruct them as the experimental networks, can be deconstructing, how to understand, how these possibilities, how the possibilities of these possibilities can be developed. Just as the code transformed cultural literacy at one time, today the networks require their specific literacy, that teach us to read the world not line by line but link by line. The birth of the networks as a model can be traced back to an enigma of the eighteenth century, in the city of Königsberg: was it possible to find a path through Königsberg that crossed each of the seven bridges once without repeating any of them? When the mathematician Leonhard Euler solved this problem, the question turned from physical geography to a new way of seeing the connections, giving rise to what he himself called the "geometry of the position" (Sachs, Stiebitz and Wilson 1988; Euler 1988; Euler 1953). The solution showed that what counted was not the real distance of the world, but how the bridges connected the different parts of the city himself to the different parts of the city. This discovery created the bases for the topology and the theory of the graphs, revealing that the elements of the territory could form schemes independently of the world with the surroundings, but as well as the bridges of the nir, but the bridges connected with the different parts of the different parts of the different parts of the different parts of the different parts of the city of the city. In this network of the different parts of the different parts of the different parts of the city. In this way of the city, Euler, the network, the network was applied to be applied to the different parts of the different parts of the different parts of the different parts of the various of the different structures of the different parts of the structures of the different structures of the structure of the city. This discovery created the different from the different parts of the different parts of the various of the different parts of the different of the city, as well, as well, and the different structures of the way, and the different of the way, and the city, as well, and the way, and the network, and the network, and applied the way, and applied the way, and the way, and the way, and the way, and the way, and the way, was created the way, and the way, and the way, and the way of the way, and the way of the way of the way of the way of the way of the way of the way, and the way, and the way of the way of the way of the Moreno and his collaborator Helen Hall Jennings asked the students to indicate the compañeros with whom they preferred to sit. They then represented these preferences in diagrams, laying the foundations for modern analysis of social networks (Scott 2000). These sociograms showed how social norms could be visualized, making otherwise abstract bonds readable. As Moreno himself said, if we could map an entire city or nation, we would discover a vast solar system of intangible structures that influence behavior with the same force with which gravity acts on bodies in space (Venturini, Munk and Jacomy 2019). Moreno argued that, until we see these structures, we work blindly in an attempt to solve social problems. These first sociograms were not yet called "networks," but they marked the first formal analysis of social relations through diagrams, opening the way to modern network visualization. Some decades later, Pierre Bourdieu distinguished himself for having integrated computational methods in social studies, going beyond purely descriptive or qualitative approaches. In *Distinctions of the social relations, opening up to the modern visualization of the network. These diagrams revealed how the symbolic boundaries of taste and distinction overlapped with structural inequalities, offering a visual account of the stratification that completed its broader sociological theory. It was also one of the first sociologists to interpret network visualizations, showing how visual forms could unveil imitation and differentiation schemes (Romele and Rodighiero 2020). On this legacy contemporary scholars are grafted that support how network visualization offers epistemical evidences, allowing interpretations of social phenomena that go beyond fixed categories and traditional schemes (Manovich 2018). These developments show how computational networks provide empirical evidence that deepen our understanding of the complex dimensions of social. From that moment on, the networks developed in parallel with the increase of computational power. The scholars began to compare themselves with increasingly large and complex datasets, passing from small scale studies to advanced resources. An influential group of researchers of the Boston area through the emergence of a manifesto that called for the development of the vast and complex Barzasi-wide network, highlighting a wide range of large-scale models that required advanced resources. In the meantime, in Europe, the philosopher Bruno Latour was invited to create a new laboratory in Paris at the University of Sciences Po, the médialab, which united sociology and computer science using design as an element of connection between the two disciplines (Boullier 2018; Venturini et al. 2017). Latour's contribution was incorporated into its wider intellectual project: the Actor-Network Theory, which redefined the society in networks of heterogeneous anchors whose relations are constantly negotiated (Latour 2005). As Harman (2009) observes, the approach of Latour was not only methodological but also ontological, treating the networks as a way to understand how reality itself is assembled. This approach gave the médialab a distinctive character, placing the networks as both conceptual and practical tools for exploring social complexity through digital and visual means. When the networks began to circulate in the public discourse and in the media, their visualization became a way of interpretation. As Löwgren and Stoltergenteries were supported by the tools that were produced by the Manterman and Stolman systems. This founding diagram shows how the dynamic dynamic sociometry unveils of affiliation and exclusion within communities (Moreno 1934)." %} In *Visual Complexity*, Manuel Lima notes that an information designer organizes data to help users see (Lima 2011, 12), stressing how design choices define what networks communicate. The design of a network begins with a research question, followed by an empirical synthesis that translates a social dimension into a relational structure. The designers represent objects as nodes and relationships as connections, focusing on the aspects of the society they want to highlight, which means choosing what to show and what to leave out. Once connected objects, metrics can express the intensity of relationships: stronger weights produce stronger attractions prepared in the layouts.Moreno's sociograms, for example, were created by interviews that asked how you like to sit down? (Moreno 1934). If these first diagrams were drawn to hand, today the data of Moro's layouts are often taken up by the data of the evidence in the form of digital charts. The layouts force-directed, for example, highlight specific aspects of the data revealing clusters, hierarchies or flows (Jacomy et al. 2014). These algorithms are central in the network analysis: developing the attraction forces between the nodes, creating spatial configurations in which the nearest positions indicate stronger relationships, helping those who observe to recognize clusters and connections. As Venturini, Jacomy and Jensen (2021) emphasize, the layouts introduce an intrinsic ambiguity: they do not map an objective space, but build one in which the relationships become visible through the logic of design. However, this ambiguity is not a limit but a resource, since it offers a subjective perspective to visually explore complex social structures. Embracing this subjectivity, an accurate design of the layout reveals hidden patterns, clarifies complexity and makes the networks more engaging: well away from being a neutral technical passage, it becomes a fundamental design decision that guides the way in which the public interprets the data. Layouts are often rendered to the user through programming, but to make the deployments of the network more accessible. It also offers intuitive tools to refine the appearance, allowing you to adjust details such as the spacing of nodes, the opacity of the bonds and the style of the labels, so as to obtain clearer visualizations (Grandjean 2015). The refinement of elements such as avoiding the overlap of nodes, the addition of curved arches and the choice of effective chromatic palettes transforms denser graphs into more readable and interesting network maps. These digital tools not only improve clarity, but also favor a deeper understanding of the network dynamics, allowing the designers to adjust iterably the way in which the data is presented. Combining analytical metrics with interactive visualizations, tools like Gephi transform networks from static diagrams to exploratory interfaces that guide the user through a more complex visualized visualization data. For the publication, Web Framework like D3.js open up new possibilities to create engaging network visualizations. The reactive programming environment of Observable automatically updates the visualizations to varying data or code, making it easier to experiment, communicate intuitions and create dynamic explanations. Together, D3.js and Observable can transform complex networks into accessible and engaging web experiences, allowing designers to transform intricate relational data into clear and exploratory stories on different devices and browsers. In a more technical context, the techniques of reduction of dimensionality such as t-SNE (van der Maaten and Hinton 2008) and UMAP (McInnes, Healy and Melville 2018) allow the designers to simplify high-dimensional data into more readable layouts to two or three dimensions, capable of revealing hidden patterns. Projecting complex datasets to visualize visualizations in two or three dimensions, these methods make it possible to visualize great similarities and groupings between data points that would otherwise be hidden in high-dimensional space. t-SNE excels in preserving local relations, capturbing visualizations to the finest structures in the data, while providing a larger scale of the most detail, while offering a balance of the same data to the most detail. t-s. As these design and methodological choices show, each step in the network visualization naturally leads us to consider how space molds the meaning. Space arrangements guide the way in which observers interpret network structures, transforming table data into visual patterns. Understanding networks as designed spaces prepares us to explore how spatial thought affects perception, revealing not only what the networks contain, but also how they communicate intuitions through their spatial organization. {% includes figure.html src="/images/network-literacy-how-to-understand-design-and-read-a-visual-relational-model/fig_002.webp" capture="Figure 2. The Grounded AI Map, a large-scale network visualization that translates two million scientific articles on artificial intelligence into a tramplable installation of 100 m2. The clusters are noted with language models of large size, allowing visitors to explore and question algorithmic knowledge through interactive bots and to map out of 2016 the dedicated (Fico 138) space-based networks. Rereading the Königsberg bridges of Euler through the lens of cultural topology shows that the networks are not only logical structures, but dynamic spaces in which relations, historical contexts and social forces interweave, demonstrating how spatial arrangements can bring cultural meanings and reflect powers and identity in transformation (Shields 2012). Beyond the theoretical perspectives, mathematical metrics offer essential tools for reading and interpreting networks, including density, degree and modularity (Grandjean 2021). The density measures how many connections exist compared to the maximum possible, giving an idea of how dense a network is. The degree, the measure of centrality simpler, indicates how many connections each node possesses, highlighting the most active or connected elements locally. Modularity detects communities identifying the regions of the network in which the nodes are more densely connected to each other than to the rest of the graph, bringing out clusters that can correspond to visual interpretations. Together, these metrics transform complex visual patterns into quantifiable intuitions, enriching the better understanding of the nodes in which are more densely connected with each other parts of the globe, since the most complex features of the centrality and interpretations of which are particularly pervading aspects of the spatial network. However, when the nodes appear near the center of a visualization, they are often interpreted as more important, even if their positioning comes from algorithmic layouts rather than objective hierarchies. In the Affinity Map, for example, the people placed at the center were perceived as more valued, as if centrality indicated prestige, even if the map reflected affinity and non-formal status (Rodigiero 2021). This shows how centrality in the network maps can be interpreted, influencing the way people see themselves and others within a network. Interactivity further improves readability, transforming static visualizations into dynamic spatial instruments of exploration. As Grandjean (2021) stresses, interactive functions such as the selection of nodes, filtering of links and zooms in dense areas allow us to directly manipulate the network, bringing out structures or relationships that could remain hidden in a static layout. This practical involvement supports active learning, recalling the idea of Piaget's visualization in the complex areas of the city, which allows us to visualize the most profoundly spaces of visualization, thus allowing us to be manipulate the networks or to be transformed into more basic networks. As Latour explains (2013), the networks are not limited to representing relationships; they create spaces of interpretation in which the meaning emerges through connections and distances. With the zoom in the clusters we can understand why certain nodes are close; with the zoom out we can ask ourselves why the clusters appear together or separate, each perspective offers a different narrative. This reading is intrinsically subjective: although there may be shared general patterns, each exploration reflects questions, interests and individual times. A personal and performative involvement makes the reading of a network an interpretative act, but it also brings with it some biases: our motivations shape what we see and how we understand the spatial history of the network. To illustrate these ideas in practice, the case of subsequent study, Surprise Machines, explores how the visualizations of the experimental network activate the spatial thought and demonstrate our expectations. {% includes figures.html src_003.network-literacy-how-to-design-and-read-jail-visual structures. The project transforms more than 200,000 images into a dynamic visual interface that reveals the often invisible scale of museum archives. Rather than simply presenting data, the project transforms network visualization into a performance and immersive experience, encouraging visitors to question what it means to see and understand a collection in its entirety. By mapping images into a two-dimensional space based on visual similarity, *Surprise Machines* combines aesthetic choices and data-driven algorithms, creating an interface at the same time scientifically rigorous and artistically evocative. This hybrid approach places the project at the crossroads between design, technology and museums, broadening what the aesthetics of the networks can mean beyond static graphics, towards interactive, incarnate and surprising encounters with cultural data. The conceptual foundation of *Surprise Machines* is to question the traditional expectations of museum exhibitions, using the networks to generate visual landscapes in continuous change. Inspired by the concept of the Alan images can produce images through the exploration of images. This calls into question the conventional view of the museum as a static repository of knowledge and instead proposes it as a fluid space of personal interpretation. By deliberately embracing ambiguity and surprise, the project invites viewers to reflect on how algorithms and human choices shape what is seen, offering a new paradigm of museum experience rooted in reticular thought. In its core, *Surprise Machines* is based on a sophisticated technical process combining layout algorithms with intended visual rules to create emerging patterns. Using tools such as PixPlot (Duhaime [2017] 2021), the project maps images based on their visual resemblance, producing a sort of reticular nebula in which groups form organically. This computational approach deviates from the traditional layout force-directed element by placing images in a space defined by their aesthetic features, thus allowing the formation of unexpected configurations. This visual rules further guides the experience: the previews of images are spaced to avoid overlapping, the plant-oriented groups maintain consistency and ensure smoothing of the landscape. Unlike the traditional visualizations, which prescribe a narrative, invites visitors to make their own readings, deciding where to zoom, which clusters investigate and what patterns to follow. This participatory model agrees with the idea that visual literacy does not only imply the recognition of the patterns, but also the critical questioning of the processes and choices behind what is made visible. Transforming navigation itself into a form of production of meaning, *Surprise Machines* empowers visitors to build personal interpretations, recognizing that every act of seeing is shaped by curiosity, experiences and individual context. This approach democratizes access to complex collections, encouraging deeper involvement and reflective encounters with data and design. Thus, challenging passive consumption of information and promotes an active attitude and question at the heart of visual literacy. Finally, *Surprise Machinery* combines spatial and design decisions in a way that highlights the passive consumption of information and promotes an active attitude and question at the heart of visual literacy. This performative aspect embodies the core of network literacy: to understand that the meaning in networks comes not only from data and design, but also from how users cross, manipulate and interpret these complex structures. *Surprise Machines* presents itself as a testimony of how a spatial and interactive conscious design can transform network visualizations into immersive and interpretative experiences. {% includes figures.html src="/images/network-literace-how-to-understand-design-and-read-a-visual-relational-model/fig_004.webp" capture="/images/network-literace-to-understand-design-to-understand-design-design-and-read-a-visual-relational-model/fig_004.webp" capture="Figure 4. Visualization of Surprise-literacy-how-literacy-how-to-understand-design-design-to-to-understand-design-of-design images of the Harvard Art Museums, which map, which map the visualized, map, map, map, map, map, map, map, map, and map, map, map, and map, map, and map, map, and landscape, and landscape, and landscape, and landscape, and landscape, and landscape, as well-read, reveal these spatial-read, the spatial-read, in the spatial-read, and space-read-read-read-read-read-read-read-read-read-read-read-of-of-of-of-the-of, and/;, and/, and/, and/;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;;; One of the most recent developments in this panorama is the use of artificial intelligence to expand the way in which networks are produced and read. Language models can now automatically label clusters, translating dense relational structures into more interpretable categories (Ficozzi et al. 2025). This opens up the possibility of multi-scale interpretation, in which different zoom levels reveal labels adapted to the granularity of visualization. McInnes' Toponymymy project illustrates this approach well: it incorporates contextual semantics in clustering, allowing a hierarchical annotation that dynamically adapts while users switch from global overviews to local details (McInnes 2025). Together, these techniques reconfigure the balance between human interpretation and automatic annotation: if on the one hand they offer new ways to extend the analysis on vast corpora, from the other raises questions on what types of meaning are introduced, obscured or distorted through algorithmic labelling (Munk et al. 2024). Cultivating the literacy of networks, therefore, not only to learn from the creatively learned to understand the same thing, but also through the computerized language of the computerized and to read the computerized language of the language. ==References====External links==* Official website ==References====External links==* Official website ==References====External links==* Official website* Official website* Official website ==References====External links==* Official website* Official website - Venturini, Tommaso, Mathieu Jacomy, Axel Meunier, and Bruno Latour. 2017. immaginare-gesti-barriera-contro-il-rito.english: It may be inappropriate to look at the post-crisis era when health workers are still at the forefront of the crisis, millions of people are losing their jobs, and many mourning families cannot even bury their deaths. Yet, this is precisely the time to fight so that economic recovery, once the crisis has ended, does not bring us back to the same old climate regime against which we have tried, without success, to fight against. Indeed, the health crisis is included in what is not a crisis, always passing by definition, but rather a lasting and irreversible ecological mutation. If we have good chances of exiting from the first, we have very few of them to escape from the second. The two situations are not on the same scale of magnitude, but it remains illuminating articulate the one with the other. In any case, it would be a sin not to reflect on the health crisis to discover other ways of entering into the ecological mutation, rather than doing it to blindly. The two situations are not at the same scale of our life, but it is still the same thing of the re-realization, and even the most surprising fact that one with the other of the other with the environmental system. This is true for the CO2 that heats the global atmosphere through its spread in the air; for the migratory birds that carry new forms of influence; but it is also true, we still learn it painfully and painfully, for the coronavirus, whose ability to connect all human beings is passing through the seemingly harmless intermediary of our Province. Viruses are super-globalizers: when it comes to re-socializing billions of human beings, the viruses do it quickly. Here then the incredible discovery: there was really in the world's economic system, hidden at all, a signal of live red alarm with a large steel handle tempered that the heads of state, each in turn, could pull out immediately to stop the progress with a strong streaked brakes. If the demand of having even better landed on the earth for a better than ever to have landed. The globalizers, those who since the middle of the 20th century have invented the idea of fleeing from the planetary bonds, also see in it a formidable opportunity to break even more radically with what hinders them in their escape from the world. For them, it is too beautiful an opportunity to get rid of the rest of the social state, the security network of the poorest, what remains of the anti-pollution regulations and, more cynically, to get rid of all these supernumerable people who engulf the planet. We do not forget, in fact, that we must assume that these globalizers are aware of the ecological changes and that all their efforts, from fifty years to this part, are directed not only to deny the importance of the climatic changes, but also to escape its consequences constituting fortified bastions of privileges that must remain inaccessible to all those who are good to leave behind. In the great modernist dream of the universal sharing of the Moscow fruit of progress, they are not naive to believe us, but the novelty is that they are the most sincious situation for those who are not even to change from the New World. We must not forget that what makes the globalizers so dangerous is that they obviously know that they have lost, that the negation of climate change cannot last indefinitely, that there is no more possibility of reconciling their development with the various spheres of the planet in which it will be necessary to end up in order to insert the economy. This is what makes them ready to try anything to obtain, one last time, the conditions that will allow them to last a little longer, to protect themselves and their children. The blockage of the world, this braking, this unexpected pause, offers them the opportunity to escape faster and farther than they have ever imagined. The revolutionaries, at the moment, are them. It is here that we must act. If the opportunity opens to them, this braking, this unexpected pause also opens to us. If everything is stopped, everything can be put back into question, flexo, selected, interrupted for real or, on the contrary, accelerated. It is now the time to make the annual inventory. At the request given by the good sense: we are able to re-start the last day the production to be done as quickly as we could. But then the camera withdrew, showing that its tulips made them grow off the ground under artificial light before delivering them to the cargo planes of Schiphol in a kerosene rain; from there arises a doubt: "But is it really useful to prolong this way of producing and selling this kind of flowers?." Gradually, if we begin, each on his own, to ask such questions on all aspects of our production system, we become effective switches of globalization           , millions as we are, as the famous coronavirus in its way of globalizing the planet. What the virus gets from the humble sputum that goes from mouth to mouth , the suspension of the world economy , we begin to imagine it through our small insignificant gestures facts, even them, one by one: namely the suspension of the production system of pixels, but it might not affect us all to imagine gestures-barriera not only against the virus, but against every element of a mode of production that we do not want to be reprovoke the single moment of the process. This does not mean to decrease or live on bread and water, but to learn to select every segment of this famous system falsely irreversible, to question each of the connections given for essentials, and to experience step by step what is desirable and what has ceased to be. Hence the capital importance of using this time of confinement imposed to describe, first alone, then in a group, what we are bound to be connected to; the one from which we are ready to free ourselves; the channels that we are ready to rebuild and those that, with our behavior, we are determined to interrupt. The globalizers, they, seem to have a very clear idea of what they want to see born after the recovery: the same thing in worse, oil industries and giant cruise ships in the first place. It is up to us to stand at home with a counter-inventory. If in a month or two, billions of humans will be able, at the flight, to learn the new, well-off social distance, to be more and more solid, to stay at home with the hospitals, we can well imagine the potential of transformation of these small-table people who will be able to prove themselves to be directly, and will be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to find a new, even more easily, even more easily, even to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to be able to Only then, if you give yourself the time to combine the answers in a landscape characterized by the overlapping of descriptions, will you be able to reach a political expression embodied and concrete "not before." Attention: it is not a questionnaire and it is not even a survey. It is a support to the self-description. It is a question of making the list of the activities you have felt deprived of from the current crisis and that give you the feeling of a violation of your essential subsistence conditions. For each activity, indicated if you would prefer them to resume as before, better than before, or that they do not resume their own. Answer these questions: 1. What are the activities currently suspended that you would not want to start again? 2. Described to) why these activities seem harmful/superfluous/dangerous/incoherent; b) to what extent would their disappearance/expected/replaced activities would make it easier/more coherent to carry out other activities that you would prefer? (Do you write a separate paragraph for each of the answers to question 1). 3. What measures would you envisage to facilitate the transition to other activities for all workers should be employed by other than those who would still wish to be employed. What measures would you take to help workers/employees/agents/entrepreneurs acquire the skills/gains/instruments to allow the recovery/development/birth of these activities? Finally, find a way to compare your description with that of other participants. The drafting and overlapping of responses should draw up a landscape consisting of conflicting lines, alliances, disputes and contrasts.

vers-une-litteratie-des-donnees-dans-les.english: Towards a data literacy in the public space: mapping the coronavirus in Ars Electronica 2021. The datafication process that is transforming society allows us to observe the pandemic from a global point of view. This article provides an example of immersive architecture in which the scientific literature on the coronavirus was revealed during the Ars Electronica 2021 festival. Like a starry sky, a network visualization representing more than 600,000 articles was presented in the Deep Space 8K theatre, where spectators were accompanied in the subject. The case study of the VOCID-19 3D Cartography illustrates a new way of presenting the data in the public space to encourage conversations and reflections, thus inviting the reader to ask about the new forms of experimental museology related to the data. > See, that's believe. It's the most credible and powerful sense we have. We need to see things. We want to see things. — Heino Falcke's other researchers have made a contribution to the disease, we have made to the science council of the 20EHT. (Galison) In the 20EHT. We need to see things. We want to see things. — Heino Falcke's other people have made a contribution to the way to the disease, we made to the last time, we made to the science of the 20EHT. For example, sociologists have studied the ritual of applause to thank health personnel (Rigal and Joseph-Goteiner 2021), and historians have compared it with the cholera epidemic of the 19th century (Huber 2020). In this unprecedented global emergency situation, data visualization and information design practitioners have tried to capture the pandemic in their own way. Coronavirus disease has produced an event characterized by a continuous flow of data, the magnitude of which is perhaps unprecedented. The information and data designers have started to translate, for paper and digital newspaper readers, the data into visuals to summarize the evolution of infection. The New York Times, for example, has created visualizations of avant-garde data that have made the one more than once a year (Coleman 2021). The pandemic has been felt at two scales: on a global level through data and locally, with a more personal dimension through stimuli such as simple telephone calls and simens of digital terms of the public's wide range of information, has been given a broad range of data, and has made the world's wide range of data. Nearly three years after the appearance of the first cases of VOCID-19, institutions continue to collect data to make them public so that they can be reused (Wilkinson et al. 2016). For example, Johns Hopkins University's contribution has been exemplary: its students have put in place an information system concerning many aspects of VOCID-19, including a vast set of data on scientific literature (Wang et al. 2020). They have also designed data visualizations to understand the complexity of the pandemic visually. Among these, the most well-known representation is undoubtedly the contamination map, which is still engraved in the memory of many of us (see Figure 1). {% include figure.html class="full" src="/images/immersive-architectures-for-visual-data-literacy/fig_001.webp" caption="Figure 1. The dashboard created by Johns Hopkins University has accompanied us throughout the period of the pandemic. {% include this class="full" src="/images/immersive-architectures-for-for-visual-data-literacy/fig_001.webp"caption=Figure 1.The dashboard created by Johns-of-of-of-sciences-for-for-for-for-for-for-for-for-for-for-for-for-for-sciences-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s-s- All scientific paper data from Johns Hopkins University was analyzed using advanced natural language automatic processing techniques (Manning and Schütze 1999) and mapped using a hybrid representation model based on network visualization (Moon and Rodighiero 2020) and topographic projection (Rodighiero and Romele 2022) to represent the most active researchers on the issue. The result obtained is a network visualization entitled "VOCID-19 mapping", which presents approximately 5,000 researchers based on the similarity of their lexical field (see Figure 2). In the visual, dozens of keywords serve as benchmarks for subjects treated by the articles, and the topographic superposition indicates the level of researcher activity; a more detailed explanation of data visualization is provided by Rodighiero, Wandl-Vogt and Carsenat (2022). Although she was rewarded at the 2020 DH Awards as "better visualization of data, but the topographical superposition indicates a more detailed explanation of the data visualization is provided by Rodighiero, Wandl-Vogt and Carsenat (2022). Although this research work has helped to identify the most active researchers and the most popular subjects, the absence of an integral representation of the scientific community has reduced the experience of the sublime of visualization, by recusing the feeling of wonder and deep admiration often felt in the face of complex scenes or images that transcend our usual understanding (Manovich 2008). {% include figure.html src="/images/immersive-archives-for-visual-data-literacy/fig_002.webp" capture="Figure 2. This network visualization represents the linguistic structure of 5,000 researchers working on the coronavirus. The digital interface allows users to zoom in on specific areas of interest to reveal the identity of researchers hidden behind the keywords. The interface and its code are available online at https://rodighero.github.io/COVID-19/%} The opportunity to provide a second response to the VOCID-19 has been provided by means of a high-level, so-called "respecta for the character's character's character and the character's character's character is the character of the character's character of the character's character of the character's character's character. One way to deal with this fear is to make tangible a phenomenon that is invisible. The project aims to reveal the significant scientific work that doctors, researchers and academics are doing to combat the disease caused by the VOCID-19 by using the visualization of data in the public space to promote understanding and exchange between spectators. (Papaki 2020) The idea was elegantly simple: to visualize the scientific literature on the coronavirus to raise awareness of the collective effort of doctors, researchers and academics. However, a significant volume of 600,000 scientific articles was gathered each year by Johns Hopkins University when the proposal was accepted, exceeding by far the 5,000 researchers previously displayed. The idea was to overcome the limit of calculation thanks to the technology provided by Ars Electronica, an Austrian institute active in the field of the art of new media and which organizes an annual festival in Linz. The institute houses the Ars Electronica Center, a museum whose primary function is to offer interactive experiences at the crossroads of art, technology and scene of the society. The character of the 2020-fold scene creates a character of the 2020-fold scene of the scene. The character of the 2020-fold scene is a character of the scene. In addition to all these exceptional features, Deep Space 8K's advanced technology was the only way to present 600,000 coronavirus-related publications in a unique and complex data visualization. {% include figure.html class="full" src="/images/immersive-architectures-for-visual-data-literacy/fig_003.webp" capture="Figure 3. This schema summarizes the organization of Deep Space 8K in the Ars Electronica Center. Eight ceiling projectors allow projection on a large surface on the ground and on the walls of the stage. Visitors experience immersive visual experience from surrounding balconies or seats positioned at the entrance."%} Although 600,000 graphic elements exceed the computing capacity of Internet browsers, Deep Space 8K's hardware allowed us to overcome this technical limit by using Unity, a video game engine widely used for iOS and Android. The data processing process was similar to the VOCID-19 Cartography, although the code was rewritten in Python, and the S.D.F.F.S.F.'s method of processing all of the data, and the S.F.F.F.F.S.S.F.S.F.S.F.S.S.S.S.S.F.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S.S. After which the multiple dimensions of the TF-IDF results were reduced by t-SNE, a dimensionality reduction algorithm capable of spatializing a large number of elements (Van der Maaten and Hinton 2008), particularly compared to the force-based graphic drawing systems (Bostock, Ogievetsky and Heer 2011) already used in the VOCID-19 Cartography. The use of t-SNE is recognizable by the cluster scheme typically associated with such an algorithm: the network visualization is organized by clusters composed of scientific articles referring to the same subject. From an aesthetic point of view, the most significant difference from the previous response is the Unity function allowing elements to be displayed in a three-dimensional space. In this respect, this additional dimension was used to illustrate the temporal evolution of the scientific literature, as a chronological frieze. This ingenious means was particularly useful to highlight articles published before the pandemic, since few people knew that the optical character of the physicists was used in the physico-form, the physicists were also used in the physico-form. Just as the maps of the stars allow astronomers to identify constellations (Kanas 2012), revealing the designated relationships between the stars, the 3D Cartography of the VOCID-19 shows the thematic groupings and evolution of scientific literature. Figures 4, 5 and 6 show three screenshots of the 600,000 scientific publications collected by Johns Hopkins University until June 2021, the date on which the 3D Cartography of the VOCID-19 was made. The three-dimensional model moves from one figure to the other to transcribe the transition between the front view, organized by language similarity, and the lateral view that follows a chronological logic. {% include figure.html src="/images/immersive-archives-for-visual-data-literacy=004.webp" caption="Figure 4. This screenshot shows how the scientific articles are organized in thematic groups. The floating key words in white before each group indicate the content. {% include-archives-for-visual-data-archives-archives-for-visual-archivities-for-archivities-for-archivities. Although most of the scientific documents are dated with the help of day, month and year, some have only one year of publication. This lack of precision created two large flat surfaces representing 2020 and 2021, cutting vertically the three-dimensional space. The spectacular view created by the impreciseness of the data, which reminds us of the Milky Way, convinced us to refrain from simulating the dates of publication."%} In the line of the previous work, such network visualization was then called the 3D Cartography of research on VOCID-19 (Ars Electronica 2021). The fascination for the visualization of large data sets in the public space is gaining ground in the digital humanities, including Sarah Kenderdine among her principal representatives. Kenderdine is a professor of digital museology at the Federal Polytechnic School of Lausanne, where she directs the laboratory of experimental museology (eM+) and the space of exhibition EPFL Pavilions. In the article *Immersive Visualization Architectures and Situated view of the digital sites,9. Kenderdine is a professor of digital museology at the digital space. This model of collective consumption is linked to Bruno Latour's pioneering work, described in the article *From Realpolitik to Dingpolitik: Or How to Make the Things Public*, which opens the catalogue of the ZKM exhibition *Making Things Public: Atmospheres of Democracy* (Latour and Weibel 2005). By using the term of assembly to translate the German concept *ding* by Heidegger, Latour illustrates a new way of seeing politics, organized no longer by political or ideological positioning but by subjects of interest. In the text, it puts forward two questions to develop this model: "The first question draws a kind of digital place, sometimes a circle, that one could call an assembly, a gathering, a meeting, a council; the second question introduces into this newly created place a subject, a concern, an issue, a problem, a *topos*" (Latour 2005, 6). "The first question draws a kind of digital place, sometimes a circle, that one could call an assembly, a meeting, a council; the second question introduces into this newly created place a "topos*" (Latour 2005, 6). Such immersive architecture makes it possible to welcome people both as contemplators and as debaters to draw attention to a specific subject and stimulate public debate. The Deep Space 8K Theatre of the Ars Electronica Center thus becomes a gathering place for individuals to discuss a specific subject. Visitors who enter this immersive space discover all the scientific literature on the VOCID-19, presented in the form of a network adorning the walls of the theatre. They are gathered in a public space to observe and reflect on a specific subject through a dynamic setting of data. The theatre, a traditional place of performance, acts as a mediator to make visible what would otherwise be invisible and becomes the place of another type of performance. Like Rudolf Arnheim (1969, v), who considered that reasoning and visual perception were intimately related, immersive architectures prolong the sensory experience of information in a place where visitors are invited to reflect collectively. If thought is a collective act, then the dialogue is the unique medium of a collective group of persons, even through which the conversation is intimately connected, the immersive architecture does not argue for itself. This collective way of consuming information around a subject represents a new form of learning involving individuals and data in a common space: individuals are gathered around a subject and the space itself provides the information to be discussed. The importance of making information visible in the public space lies in the opportunity to share opinions and avoid the passivity of information that characterizes the consumption of information about personal devices. Digital media cannot be limited to a personal place of discussion, but must also extend to a space that people can occupy, traverse and invest, to develop a collective literacy of data. The concept of data literacy underscores the extent to which educational systems are anchored in text and writing, and that new skills to understand data (Ignazio and Bhargava 2015) and their infrastructure (Gray, Gerlitz and Bounegru 2018) are needed of all urgency. In this period of information explosion (Balazka and Rodighiero,voy,voyen,voyen,voyen,voyen,voyen,voyen,voyen,voyen,voyen,voyen,voyen,voyez,voy,voy,voy,voy,voy,voy,voy,voy,voy,voy,voy,voy,voy,voy,voy,voy,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo,vo, In this sense, the definition of data literacy should be extended to visual literacy, a concept already developed in the past by Professor Donis Dondis of the University of Boston (Dondis [1973] 1975) and taken up more recently by Isabel Meirelles (2013, 9). Although László Moholy-Nagy, Dondis and Meirelles emphasized the need for an education in graphic design at different times, today there is an opportunity to combine visual pedagogy with a critical mind in the interpretation of data in what can be called the visual literacy of data. When she writes about literacy, Catherine D I engage in dialogue because I recognize the social and not merely individualistic character of the knowledge process. In this sense, dialogue is presented as an indispensable component of the learning and knowledge process. (Freire [1970] 2000, 17) Dialogical practice plays an important role in immersive architectures where the exposed information is not left to the visitors' disposal but requires educational activity. For example, the 3D Cartography of research on the VOCID-19 was not only a technical installation but was first and foremost an event in which the spectators acquired knowledge to interpret the data, giving them the essential tools to form their own opinion. The "Cartography of research on the VOCID-19" was presented during the 19th Ars Electronica 2021. On 9 September, at 10 o'clock, visitors to the Ars Electronica center entered into the 8K Deep Space. The event began with a rapid presentation of the project partners and continued with a 20 minute description of the visualization of the data. For example, the SARS epidemic between 2002 and 2004 was used to introduce chronology: the disproportion between the amount of the scientific literature of the SARS epidemic and that of the pandemic was immediately evident to the public, thus helping spectators to get an idea of the impact of the VOCID-19 on the world of research. Finally, this direct presentation was followed by a dialogue with the authors of the visualization in which the spectators raised a wide range of issues mainly related to artificial intelligence and the future of immersive architecture. Such a moment of confrontation was a continuation of Freire's dialogical practice, which is essential for society to discuss and understand. The concept of visual data literacy based on this pedagogical approach should not be limited to schools and universities, but should be extended to public space, whose museums are an excellent example and vector. {% include figure.html src=/images/immersive-architectures-for-visual-figures9.cypt=enough/informat-literacy/fig.webp=encrypt_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en__en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en___en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en_en__en_en_en_en_en_en__en_en_en___en_en____en_en_en___en_en_en_en__________________________________________________en_en______ Through a rotation of visualization in the three-dimensional space, visitors observed the temporal dimension of scientific literature on two flat surfaces corresponding to the years 2020 and 2021."%} {% include figure.html src="/images/immersive-archives-for-visual-data-literacy/fig_009.webp" capture="Figure 9. Spectators observed the visualization of data in front of them. Although the projection on the ground was not used due to technical limitations, the immersive effect was still fascinating, as seen by spectators."%} This article summarizes the effort to make visible the invisible work of scientists by showing the scientific publications of the VOCID-19 to the general public at an art festival that reopened its doors for the first time after the pandemic. As Katy Börner and his peers have declared, museum visitors still encounter difficulties in reading data visualizations when they are not engaged in dialogical practice (Börner et al. 2016). In addition, the design of places to gather individuals around data-oriented topics deserves attention, not only for the subject in itself, but also, more broadly, to promote the visual literacy of data among citizens in the public space.

surprise-machines-visualizzare-le-immagi.english: Surprise Machines: view the archive images of the Harvard Art Museums. Surprise Machines is a project of experimental museums that makes visible the set of archive images preserved in the art museums of Harvard University, in English Harvard Art Museums. With the idea of making visible to museum visitors a collection of more than 200,000 objects, the research explores the potential and limits of artificial intelligence in representing a large set of images. Inserted in the Curatorial exhibition A(i) gents, organized by the metaLAB (at) Harvard, the project has the ultimate aim to amaze revealing the extension of an archive not directly accessible to museum visitors. To amplify this effect of surprise, the museum's interest is enriched by a museum's major researches, while the museum's recent advancements in the artificial intelligence. The project _Surprise Machines_ has at its center a data visualization that maps over 200,000 images of the _Harvard Art Museums_, presented through a museum installation to allow visitors to see and understand the extension of the museum archives. Part of a temporary exhibition organized by _metaLAB (at) Harvard_ and entitled _Curatorial Art Museum A(i)gents_, the visitors of the museums can interact with the visualization through a choreographic interface based on the gestures of the body. The project is unique for its interdisciplinaryity, which combines a prestigious collection of art with the latest artificial intelligence techniques. In the following sections, the text introduces the reader to: 2) the museum and its collection, 3) the curatorial philosophy of the exhibition, 4) the data visualization, 5) this software for the interaction of the Horeographical years, and 6) the technical problems faced by the research group. The history of the _Harvard Art Museums_ began in 1891 with a generous donation of the Ms. Elizabeth Fogg. Today, the collection of _Harvard Art Museums_ has exceeded 200,000 works. Despite the extensions made over the years, this continuous growth forced a complete renovation of the building, operated by the architect Renzo Piano in 2014. After the demolition of the post-1925 expansions, Piano integrates a new exhibition area facing Prescott Street to the existing Quincy Street building, adding a glass cover to bring natural light in the galleries, laboratories and the marble courtyard of Travertino. In parallel with this continuous growth of the collections, the digital has made its entrance into the museum world, increasing the archival capacity. Haidy Geismar describes this transformation with the term "contact zone" , a point where the works and technology meet in the form of online catalogues, websites and digital platforms. During this transition, for example, the _Harvard Art Museums_ space has developed a digital archive that collects almost 240,000 objects with images, titles, descriptions, assignments, classifications, collections, authors, subjects, media, objects are accessible through the space. The machine, which is considered to be an intelligent machine with the idea of connecting visitors to the digital dimension of the collection. In spring 2022, the Lightbox Gallery hosts the exhibition _Curatorial A(i)gts_ exhibition. Based on a critical approach to artificial intelligence, the exhibition presents a series of weekly installations, created by the members of the metallab. The curatorial philosophy of the exhibition appears in a brochure designed by Chelsea Qiu, which develops an original method of compositional reading based on the folds of the sheet. In its interior, the director of the metaLAB Jeffrey Schnapp reveals the meaning of _Curatorial A(i)gents_ seeking the curator by combining it with the curator through collections and programming to the neologism of the prototypes, can be used by means of a gesture of intrusion in a territory modeled by artificial intelligence. Schnapping thus responds to the question of human beings by means of the procession of the machineries. In the article, Turing develops an interesting argument that touches several examples, one of which refers to the English mathematics Ada Lovelace. By contrasting to Lovelace's thought that machines are unable to surprise us because they are unable to think, Turing states that machines are a source of constant astonishment due to their unpredictable behavior, which often surprises the user. Inspired by this sense of surprise, Surprise Machines tries to amaze visitors to the museum by presenting about 213,000 objects of the collection of Harvard Art Museums. Initially, the visitor is amazed at the realization of the grandeur of the collection; later, the visitor reflects on the small number of objects exhibited in the museum, a quick estimate from the website shows that less than one percent of the objects has been displayed over the years. From the beginning, it was thought that _Curatorial A(i)gents_ had needed a project focused on the entire collection of the _Harvard Museums_. If the visualization was visualized by the visualization of the first case of the process when it was performed on the data, however, it was thought that the data could be used on the transparent. Ten years after the publication of his book _Visual Complexity_, the panorama of the _network visualization_ (D Humanties_View_ visualization_ has changed considerably thanks to the development of a visual language to which academics known worldwide have contributed to academics known as Albert-László Barabási. At the computational level, instead, the transformations in this field have mainly affected the algorithms of _force-directed graph_ that have been partially replaced by new techniques, such as that of_dimensionality reduction_, which allow to manage more extensive networks in terms of numbers and connections. The greater complexity in viewing image collections consists in computing a large number of elements. The beginning of the development of _Surprise Machines_ has been characterized by the creation of a local data base made up of local data composed of images downloaded by means of the IIIF protocol. This operation was possible by means of the "API" provided by the museum, which allowed the _download_ of about 213,000 images, corresponding to about 50 GB of space on disk through a year through a resolution through the third-of a year, the first attempt to create a poor of a display of a visualization through a year, using the poor year, using a metric of a metric of a metric of a metric of a metric of a metric, the metric, and despite of a disc, which was based on the disc, unfortunately on the connections, which was unfortunately on the connections of which was unfortunately provided in connection on the contents, unfortunately, and unfortunately, and unfortunately, was made in connection on the contents of which was made by the contents of which it was made by the contents of which was made by the contents of which it was made by the contents of which it was made by the previous, was made by the number of which it was made by the contents, was made by the number of which was not possible, was made by the number of which it was made by the number of which it was made by the number of which was made by the number of which it was made by the number of which was made by the number of the number of the number of which was made by the number of the number of which was made by the number of several of several of which was made by the number of which was made in the number of the number of several of several of several of several of several of several of several of The DHLAB is a laboratory created to support students and researchers interested in digital methods. Among the tools made available to the group, the software _PixPlot_ represented a significant turning point for the development of _Surprise Machines_. _PixPlot_ is in fact a tool for displaying image collections that has found some interest in the scientific community for its flexibility and uniqueness. Written in two programming languages, Python and JavaScript, the software initially processes a set of images _bitmap_ through an automatic learning technique called convolutional neural network developed on the basis of data ImageNet 2012. This part of the code transforms a collection of images into numerical vectors, which are subsequently converted by the algorithm of _dimensionality reduction_ UMAP into a two-dimensional visualization. At the end of this process, the JavaScript code generates an interactive HTML interface based on WebGL technologies with which the user can interact by exploring a collection of images in the space-based groups results result in a similar to the standard image. If you look deeper in view, Figure 2 shows a detail of the visualization relative to a group of handmade portraits in which they can be recognized Mahatma Gandhi and Albert Einstein. Using a concept developed by Lev Manovich, it is important to remember that the fascination in displaying extended collections is a purely sublime act, but the magnification of the details leads to specific information as Ben Shneiderman recalls. A careful use of the interface allows the user to discover specific groups of images, such as the collection of drawings of the Bauhaus or that of the pigments in powder for the paintings. From this point on, _Surprise Machine_ leaves space for viewers, whose interpretation of the visualization becomes a necessary act to complement the design process. {% includes figures.html src="/images/surprise-machines-revealing collection of the libera. In this area that shows hand-made portraits, Mahatma Gandhi and Albert Einstein are visible at the top left of the center." %} The exhibition _Curatorial A(i)gents_ was to be inaugurated in 2020, but the pandemic forced the organizers to postpone it by about two years. In this period, uncertain of the health protocols in force at the opening, the metaLAB started a search for an interface without physical contact: of the 11 projects that composed the exhibition, in fact, about half required conventional interactions with mouse and keyboard that posed problems of virus transmission. Conceptually, the group in charge of exploring this solution transformed this limit into an opportunity, developing a choreographic interface that could allow visitors of the museum to use a gestural vocabulary _tuary to interact with the projects. Conceptually, the choreographical selection interface is a device that increases the kinetic and spatial interactivity between humans and computers integrating the choreographical thought in the design process. Several iterations were necessary in the design phase to arrive at the final result, mainly because these softwares are limited to the elaboration of extremely distinct static positions from each other. This limitation led to the development of a geometric and dissimilar gesture, attentive to the negative space around the body to make the limbs always visible to the camera. The various iterations led to a solution that balances the choreographic interest and computational legibility. The choreographic interface was influenced in particular by the _Surprise Machines_ project because, among all the existing projects, included the package of more stimulating interactions to be realized. In this context, the tracing of the hands was particularly challenging because the interpretation of the machine took place on the whole body while the focus of the interaction required to focus on the arts. _Surprise Machines_ has in fact simultaneously requested the tracing of the hand and the gestures of the torso. The first iterations were computely challenging and non-performative for a real-time operation, especially in the first test with the prototype_PixPoli_ model also increased for the higher performances. Finding some interactions uncomfortable, it has also implemented a method to change the dominant hand by simply looking at the passive hand. Wanting to offer visitors a performative experience, we have finally created an information level through the sonification of the choreographic interface. The sonification adds and modulates an audio background to the movements of the body, providing a dynamic score to the movements and strengthening the gestures of the interface. The position and acceleration of the body guide the changes in the sound with the idea of joining the physical movement to the digital one. While the choreographic interface included a visual feedback on the screen (see Figure 4), the auditory feedback freed the visual attention of the visitor to fully focus on the content of the projects. {% includes figures.html src="/images/surprise-artmachines-revealing-harvard-art-museums-collection/fig_004.webp" caption="Figure 3. L's coreographic interface invites visitors to interact with the iboltest-harvard-harb? The screen shows the interface of Trails based on a limited set of image previews, distinguishable from the white background. " %} The test phase was a delicate moment, especially considering that the project _Surprise Machines_ was developed by a team distributed in different parts of the world. Fortunately, the presence of some of us at the _Harvard Art Museums_ made possible the tests when the lockdown measures against the COVID-19 were less severe. During the first test, the team had mixed feelings. Although the graphics performance was incredably captivating, the hardware unfortunately did not support the computational load due to the high number of images. As a result, the project was remodeled by adopting a software called _Trails_ during the first test, the team had mixed feelings. Although the graphics performance was incredibly captivating, the hardware unfortunately did not support the computational load due to the high number of images. As a result, the project was remodeled by the U.Trails_ was remodeled by using a software designed by DHL for very high resolution screens. Although the test, the LAB for extremely high resolution screens for high resolution screens at high resolution, the high resolution screens. Obviously, the intention is to use the choreographic interface for future versions of _Surprise Machines_ when the hardware that drives the system can be managed more closely by the developers. The _Lightbox Gallery_ hosted _Surprise Machines_ during the week of 17-22 May 2022, equipped with a _air mouse_ to move the cursor on the screen.
